did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (specifically EfficientNet, ConvNeXt, and ECL) to evaluate their performance on the Early-Stage Melanoma Benchmark (EMB) dataset for the task of melanoma detection and T-category classification based on Breslow thickness.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper introduces a new dataset, the Early-Stage Melanoma Benchmark (EMB), and presents original experimental results by evaluating state-of-the-art deep learning models (EfficientNet, ConvNeXt, ECL) on this dataset. It includes methods for data curation, duplicate removal, and performance analysis, which are characteristic of original research rather than a review.

title: Early-Stage Melanoma Benchmark Dataset
brief_summary: This study introduces the Early-Stage Melanoma Benchmark (EMB) dataset, comprising over 1100 dermoscopic and clinical images labeled by T-category based on Breslow thickness to address the lack of stage-specific data in melanoma detection. The dataset was curated from public sources, including the ISIC archive and Dermoscopy Atlas, with rigorous filtering to remove duplicates and overlap with standard training sets. Several state-of-the-art deep learning models, including EfficientNet, ConvNeXt, and ECL, were evaluated on this dataset to assess their generalization capabilities. The evaluation revealed a significant drop in model performance on the EMB dataset compared to results on standard ISIC datasets, particularly for in situ and thin melanomas. These findings highlight the challenges of early-stage melanoma detection and demonstrate the limited robustness of current AI models when applied to unseen, stage-specific data. The EMB dataset serves as a valuable resource for cross-dataset benchmarking and developing more clinically applicable automated diagnostic systems.

lesion_types_studied.details: The EMB dataset contains 1104 images of melanoma labeled by T-category based on Breslow thickness. The study focuses on detecting and classifying early-stage melanoma (Tis, T1) versus later stages (T2-T4). Specific subtypes mentioned in the context of classification tasks include in situ, thin, and thick melanoma.
A specific T-category (Tis) representing melanoma in situ (Breslow thickness 0 mm) within the EMB dataset. Models showed lowest accuracy for this class.
A specific T-category (T1) representing thin melanoma (Breslow thickness ≤ 1.0 mm) within the EMB dataset. Models showed lowest accuracy for this class.
T2 (>1.0–2.0 mm) and T3 (>2.0–4.0 mm) categories within the EMB dataset.
A specific T-category (T4) representing thick melanoma (>4.0 mm) within the EMB dataset.

all_task_types.target: T-category based on Breslow thickness (Tis, T1, T2, T3, T4) and binary melanoma/malignant class
all_task_types.details: The paper evaluates deep learning models (EfficientNet, ConvNeXt, ECL) for classifying melanoma images into discrete T-categories (Tis, T1, T2, T3, T4) based on Breslow thickness, as well as binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma). Performance is measured by classification accuracy on these discrete categories.

datasets_used.name: Early-Stage Melanoma Benchmark (EMB)
datasets_used.description: A curated benchmark dataset of melanoma images labeled by T-category based on Breslow thickness, designed to address the lack of stage-specific data and overlap with standard training sets. It was compiled from the ISIC Archive and Dermoscopy Atlas, with duplicates removed and overlap with ISIC Challenge datasets (2016-2020) excluded.

use.testing: Used as an external benchmark to evaluate the generalization performance of state-of-the-art deep learning models (EfficientNet, ConvNeXt, ECL) that were trained on ISIC Challenge datasets.
use.other: Serves as a resource for cross-dataset benchmarking and T-category-specific analysis of early-stage melanoma detection.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A public repository of skin lesion images. Images with melanoma thickness labels and melanoma in situ categories were downloaded from this source for the construction of the EMB dataset.

use.other: Source of 907 images for the EMB dataset.

datasets_used.name: Dermoscopy Atlas
datasets_used.description: A public website containing dermoscopic images. Data was scraped from this source for the construction of the EMB dataset.

use.other: Source of 197 images for the EMB dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Challenge Datasets (2016-2020)
datasets_used.description: Standard public datasets used for training and evaluating deep learning models. The EMB dataset was specifically filtered to avoid overlap with these datasets to ensure fair cross-dataset evaluation.

use.training: Used to train the baseline models (EfficientNet, ConvNeXt, ECL) whose performance was then tested on the EMB dataset.
use.other: Used as a reference for performance comparison; overlap with EMB was removed.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the uncertainty of the median accuracy for the EfficientNet-B4 768 9c model on the EMB dataset.
To estimate the uncertainty of the accuracy for the ECL 9 class model on the EMB dataset.
To estimate the uncertainty of the accuracy for the ConvNeXt-DMP 224 2c model on the EMB dataset.
To report the central tendency of model performance on the EMB dataset for the EfficientNet-B4 768 9c model.
To measure classification accuracy for all models on the EMB dataset and its subsets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported performance metrics (accuracy) and 95% confidence intervals to estimate uncertainty, but did not perform statistical hypothesis tests to compare groups.

Early-Stage Melanoma Benchmark (EMB).description: Cross-dataset evaluation of state-of-the-art deep learning models trained on ISIC Challenge datasets (2016-2020) and tested on the EMB dataset. Clinical images were excluded from testing. Results represent the fraction of correctly predicted images (accuracy) for the entire dataset and subsets by T-category (Tis, T1, T2, T3, T4).
Early-Stage Melanoma Benchmark (EMB).notes: All tested algorithms performed well on the datasets they were originally trained on but exhibited a significant drop in performance on EMB. The best-performing model from the ISIC winner ensemble (EfficientNet-B4 768 9c) reached an accuracy of 97.71% on combined ISIC 2018-2020 data, but achieved a median accuracy of 0.54 (95% CI [0.52, 0.57]) on EMB. The ECL 9 class model showed reduced accuracy of 0.40 (CI [0.37, 0.42]) on EMB compared to 86.11% on ISIC 2019. ConvNeXt-DMP 224 2c declined to 0.55 (CI [0.52, 0.58]) on EMB from 90.70% on ISIC 2019+2020. All models showed the lowest accuracy on in situ (Tis) and T1 melanomas. Performance generally improved with increasing thickness, with T3 and T4 yielding the highest accuracy for most models. UMAP visualization revealed non-uniformity between feature representations of ISIC 2019 and EMB data. Grad-CAM visualizations indicated that models often focused on unrelated regions for Tis and T1 cases while ignoring high pigmentation areas.

architecture_used.main_model: The paper evaluated multiple state-of-the-art deep learning models: EfficientNet Ensemble (specifically EfficientNet-B4, B5, B6, B7 variants), ConvNeXt (with Deep Mask Pixel-wise Supervision, DMP), and ECL (Class-Enhancement Contrastive Learning).
architecture_used.pretraining: All models were pre-trained on ISIC Challenge datasets (2016-2020) without external data. Specifically, EfficientNet models were trained on combined ISIC 2018-2020 data. ECL 9-class model was trained on ISIC 2019. ConvNeXt-DMP models were trained on ISIC 2019+2020 data.
architecture_used.input_size: Input sizes varied by model configuration: 224x224 (ConvNeXt-DP/DMP, ECL), 448x448 (EfficientNet-B4, B5, B6), 576x576 (EfficientNet-B6, B7), 640x640 (EfficientNet-B4, B5, B6, B7, ResNeSt-101, SE-ResNeXt-101), 768x768 (EfficientNet-B4), 896x896 (EfficientNet-B4).

architecture_details.EfficientNet: Variants B4, B5, B6, B7 were tested. Models were part of the winner ensemble of the SIIM-ISIC challenge in 2020. Individual models from the ensemble were tested, excluding those utilizing metadata.
architecture_details.ConvNeXt: Used with Deep Mask Pixel-wise Supervision (DMP). Tested as 9-class and 2-class models.
architecture_details.ECL: Class-Enhancement Contrastive Learning for Long-tailed Skin Lesion Classification. Tested as 8-class and 9-class models.
architecture_details.Other Models: ResNeSt-101 and SE-ResNeXt-101 were also evaluated.

training_details.training_data: ISIC Challenge datasets (2016-2020). No external data was used for training.
training_details.preprocessing: Input images were preprocessed according to the specific requirements described in the original papers for each model.
training_details.clinical_images: Clinical images were excluded from testing as the methods were trained on dermoscopic images only.

classification_or_output_head.binary_models: 2-class models (e.g., ConvNeXt-DP 2c, ConvNeXt-DMP 2c, EfficientNet-B5 640 4c) predicted melanoma vs. non-melanoma. Since the EMB dataset contains only melanoma cases, all images should be classified as the positive class.
classification_or_output_head.multi_class_models: 9-class models (e.g., EfficientNet-B4 768 9c, ECL 9c, ConvNeXt-DMP 224 9c) predicted T-categories (Tis, T1, T2, T3, T4) and potentially other classes depending on the specific training set (e.g., non-melanoma classes if included in the original ISIC training). 8-class ECL model was also tested.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: EfficientNet Ensemble (specifically EfficientNet-B4, B5, B6, B7 variants)
Class-Enhancement Contrastive Learning for Long-tailed Skin Lesion Classification (ECL)
preprocessing_techniques: Duplicate removal using czkawka application with hamming distance comparison on Lanczos3 resized gradient hashes (size 16, similarity threshold 40)
Manual investigation of flagged duplicates to exclude false positives
Exclusion of overlapping images between the benchmark dataset and ISIC Challenge datasets (2016-2020) based on ISIC IDs and hash comparison
Exclusion of clinical images from testing as models were trained on dermoscopic images only
Input images preprocessed according to specific requirements described in the original papers for each model (EfficientNet, ConvNeXt, ECL)
conclusions: State-of-the-art deep learning models trained on standard ISIC Challenge datasets exhibit a significant drop in performance when evaluated on the new Early-Stage Melanoma Benchmark (EMB) dataset, highlighting limited generalization to unseen, stage-specific data.
Current AI models struggle specifically with detecting early-stage melanoma, showing the lowest accuracy for melanoma in situ (Tis) and thin melanoma (T1) cases.
Model performance generally improves with increasing tumor thickness, with T3 and T4 melanomas yielding the highest accuracy for most tested models.

Feature representation analysis using UMAP reveals non-uniformity between the feature distributions of ISIC Challenge data and the EMB dataset, indicating that shared features are insufficient for accurate classification of early-stage lesions.

Grad-CAM visualizations indicate that models often focus on unrelated regions and ignore high-pigmentation areas when classifying early-stage (Tis and T1) melanomas, suggesting flawed decision-making processes for these cases.

The lack of knowledge about melanoma stage distribution in training data and the scarcity of publicly available datasets with detailed Breslow thickness information limit the clinical applicability and reliability of automated melanoma diagnostic systems.

The EMB dataset serves as a valuable resource for cross-dataset benchmarking and T-category-specific analysis to assess the robustness and clinical applicability of automated melanoma diagnosis systems.
important_remarks: Significant performance drop: State-of-the-art models trained on ISIC Challenge datasets showed a substantial decline in accuracy when evaluated on the EMB dataset (e.g., EfficientNet-B4 768 9c dropped from 97.71% on ISIC to 54% median accuracy on EMB), highlighting limited generalization to unseen, stage-specific data.

Poor early-stage detection: Models exhibited the lowest accuracy for melanoma in situ (Tis) and thin melanoma (T1) cases, often focusing on unrelated regions and ignoring high-pigmentation areas, which poses a barrier to clinical applicability for early detection.

Potential data leakage: The authors acknowledge that despite efforts to remove overlap, some images in the EMB dataset may still originate from the ISIC Archive and thus appear in the training sets of the evaluated models, potentially affecting benchmarking reliability.

Dataset bias and composition: The EMB dataset is not balanced; it contains a higher representation of thin/in situ melanomas and dermoscopic images compared to clinical images. It was derived from public repositories 'as-is' and was not curated to mitigate racial bias.

Clinical image exclusion: Clinical images were excluded from testing because the evaluated models were trained exclusively on dermoscopic images, limiting the assessment of model robustness across different image acquisition modalities.

Lack of cross-dataset evaluation in field: The paper notes that cross-dataset evaluation is vastly underutilized in the field (e.g., only 23.6% of works in one study used external datasets), which reduces the reliability of AI models in real-world clinical settings.

Missing metadata in training data: A key limitation of existing datasets is the lack of detailed pathology metadata (e.g., Breslow thickness, ulceration), making it difficult to assess model performance on early-stage lesions where visual features are subtle and similar to benign nevi.
